stem: A 24-year-old hospitality worker self-presents to a community mental health team after an overdose of 12 paracetamol tablets last night following an argument with her current partner of three weeks. During the assessment interview she tells the registrar that her partner is the only person who has ever truly understood her, and that the team nurse who saw her on arrival was 'cold and judgemental', in stark contrast with 'you, the first to really listen'. She reports two prior emergency presentations in the past year following similar low-lethality overdoses, longstanding cutting of her forearms since age 15, recurrent feelings of emptiness, and intense difficulty being alone. She denies sustained elevated mood, persistent psychotic symptoms, and has no current diurnal mood variation, no early morning waking, no anhedonia outside relationship distress, and her mood reactively brightens when discussing her partner. Father describes lifelong rapid shifts between idealising and devaluing close friends since adolescence. Serum paracetamol below the treatment line at 8 hours; LFTs normal. She has had no episodes of pervasive low mood lasting beyond a few days.

data_table.Examination: Alert, mildly tearful then bright affect within the same interview, no thought disorder, no perceptual disturbance, multiple superficial healed and recent linear scars to bilateral forearms.
data_table.Investigations: Serum paracetamol below Rumack-Matthew treatment line at 8 hours. ALT 24 U/L, INR 1.0, K-10 32.

lead_in: Which clinical feature most strongly supports a primary diagnosis of borderline personality disorder OVER major depressive disorder in this presentation?

options.text: Rapid splitting of the treating team within a single assessment, in the context of lifelong pattern of idealisation/devaluation since adolescence
options.rationale: Real-time splitting (idealising the current clinician while devaluing the nurse minutes earlier) within a single assessment, combined with collateral history of the same pattern since adolescence, is the most specific feature pointing to BPD over MDD. MDD does not produce splitting; identity-and-relationships instability is core BPD. The pattern's duration since adolescence (DSM-5 onset criterion) and collateral confirmation increase specificity.

options.text: Multiple low-lethality self-harm episodes recurring since adolescence in the context of chronic interpersonal stressors
options.rationale: Recurrent self-harm is consistent with BPD but not specific (also MDD, adjustment). Not strongest discriminator. Would never be the strongest discriminator alone (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline).

options.text: Two emergency department presentations in the past month after acute relationship arguments with her current partner
options.rationale: Crisis pattern in response to relational stressors is consistent with BPD but not specific; many MDD patients present in interpersonal crisis. Would never be the discriminator (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Chronic subjective feelings of emptiness reported over many years, present across multiple interpersonal contexts
options.rationale: Emptiness is part of BPD but also reported in chronic depression; alone does not discriminate. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Mood reactivity observed during the interview, with tearful affect that brightens markedly in response to supportive engagement
options.rationale: Mood reactivity argues AGAINST melancholic MDD but does not distinguish BPD from atypical-features MDD. Would never be the BPD-vs-MDD discriminator (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: BPD is diagnosed when 5 of 9 DSM-5 criteria are met across a longstanding pervasive pattern. Splitting (alternating idealisation and devaluation) is uncommon in primary mood disorders and is the feature in this vignette that best discriminates BPD from MDD (RANZCP CPG BPD 2012).
key_points: DSM-5 BPD: 5 of 9 criteria including abandonment fears, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, recurrent self-harm/suicidal behaviour, affective lability, emptiness, anger, transient stress-related paranoia/dissociation
Splitting is highly suggestive of BPD and rare in primary mood disorders
Self-harm in BPD is typically chronic, low-lethality and interpersonally triggered
Comorbid MDD is common but does not subsume the personality diagnosis
explanation.pearls: When the team starts to feel 'split' (one clinician idealised, another devalued), reflect this back to the team in supervision - it is diagnostic data, not a personnel issue (RANZCP CPG BPD).

stem: A 26-year-old woman has a confirmed diagnosis of borderline personality disorder with recurrent self-harm by superficial cutting, three low-lethality paracetamol overdoses in the past year, and intense relationship instability with two short-lived partners. She works part time, lives with her mother, and has been seeing her general practitioner under a Mental Health Treatment Plan. Past trials of sertraline, escitalopram and quetiapine over the last 18 months produced no durable benefit. She has no current major depressive episode by SIGECAPS criteria, no psychotic features, and her current K-10 is 30. She is not pregnant, drinks under 4 standard drinks a week and has stopped occasional cannabis. She tells the GP she has read about a structured group programme and wants to know what evidence-based treatment should be offered to address the pattern of self-harm and interpersonal volatility itself, rather than just symptom relief.

data_table.Examination: Multiple healed and recent superficial linear scars to forearms. MSE: euthymic at interview, reactive affect, no psychotic features, no current suicidal intent.
data_table.Investigations: K-10 30, FBC, EUC, LFT, TSH unremarkable, urine drug screen negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment to address the core borderline personality disorder presentation?

options.text: Commence lamotrigine titrated to 200 mg daily for affective instability
options.rationale: Mood stabilisers including lamotrigine do not have strong evidence in BPD; NHMRC explicitly recommends against routine pharmacotherapy for core BPD. Would be correct only for comorbid bipolar disorder (Source: NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Structured psychological therapy such as dialectical behaviour therapy
options.rationale: Structured evidence-based psychotherapies (DBT, mentalisation-based treatment, schema therapy, transference-focused psychotherapy) are first-line for BPD per the NHMRC and RANZCP guidelines; DBT has the strongest evidence for reducing self-harm and emergency presentations (NHMRC CPG BPD; RANZCP CPG BPD).

options.text: Long-term low-dose olanzapine 5 mg nocte for emotional dysregulation
options.rationale: Second-gen antipsychotics not recommended as first-line maintenance for BPD (metabolic risk, limited evidence beyond short-term crisis). Would be correct only as short-term crisis use (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Add a third SSRI trial with extended titration to maximum dose
options.rationale: Two prior SSRI trials failed and SSRIs do not treat core BPD; further antidepressant trials reserved for clearly comorbid MDD. Would be correct only with new comorbid MDD episode (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Six-week voluntary inpatient admission for behavioural stabilisation
options.rationale: Prolonged inpatient admission not recommended for BPD (reinforces maladaptive coping); brief crisis admissions only when safety cannot be managed in community. Would be correct only for brief crisis (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder affects about 1-2% of Australians and disproportionately presents to mental health services. The NHMRC 2013 clinical practice guideline names four first-line structured psychological therapies: dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT, the most-evidenced and most-available in Australia), mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, and transference-focused psychotherapy (TFP). Medications have no first-line role; they may target comorbid mood, anxiety, psychotic or substance use disorders but should never be prescribed for the BPD itself. Polypharmacy and benzodiazepine prescription are particularly harmful. Inpatient admission is short, structured and focused on safety - long admissions reinforce maladaptive patterns and are discouraged. Crisis lines, written safety plans, and continuity of an outpatient therapeutic relationship outperform any medication. Recovery rates are higher than once believed: longitudinal data show over 80% achieve remission within 10 years.
key_points: First-line BPD: DBT or other structured evidence-based psychotherapy
Medications: treat comorbid MDD, anxiety or psychosis only; not the core disorder
Avoid polypharmacy in BPD - it accumulates harm without core benefit
Inpatient care: brief, crisis-focused, time-limited
Australian access via MBS Mental Health Treatment Plan and public DBT programmes
explanation.pearls: DBT (dialectical behaviour therapy), MBT (mentalisation-based therapy), schema therapy and TFP (transference-focused psychotherapy) are the four NHMRC-named first-line BPD treatments; medications target comorbidity only and should not be prescribed for the core disorder (NHMRC BPD 2013).

stem: A 30-year-old man is reviewed in a community mental health clinic after release from a 12-month custodial sentence for aggravated assault. Documented history: truancy from age 11, cruelty to a neighbour's cat at age 13, fire-setting at 14, multiple arrests for fraud and driving offences as a young adult, pattern of brief sexual relationships ended by him without distress. He is referred because his probation officer is concerned about his mental state. On interview he is articulate, charming, shows little remorse for the assault and frames it as the victim's fault. He denies persistent low mood, sustained elevated mood, hallucinations, recurrent self-harm, chronic emptiness, fear of abandonment, or rapid shifts in his view of others (no splitting pattern on collateral). Collateral from his sister confirms longstanding deceitfulness, irritability and callous disregard for the welfare of partners and family across at least the past decade. He drinks heavily on weekends, uses cocaine intermittently and is on no regular medications.

data_table.Examination: Calm, oriented, no thought disorder, no perceptual disturbance, MSE notable for shallow affect and lack of remorse.
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen positive for cocaine metabolites and cannabinoids. FBC, EUC, LFTs, TSH unremarkable.

lead_in: Which feature most specifically supports antisocial personality disorder OVER borderline personality disorder in this man?

options.text: Cocaine and heavy episodic alcohol use without any documented evidence of childhood conduct disorder or criminal disregard for others
options.rationale: Substance use common in both ASPD and BPD; not discriminating. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Multiple short-lived sexual relationships ended by the patient without any documented exploitation, aggression or callous disregard for others
options.rationale: Brief unstable relationships occur in both; ASPD ends out of exploitation/boredom, BPD out of abandonment fear or devaluation; relational pattern is the discriminator. Would never be the strongest discriminator alone (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Evidence of conduct disorder before age 15 (truancy, cruelty to animals, fire-setting) followed by adult callous disregard for the welfare of others and lack of remorse
options.rationale: DSM-5 ASPD criterion C requires evidence of conduct disorder with onset before age 15 (criterion A1-15 covers aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness, serious rules violations); his history meets at least three. This is a unique and discriminating criterion - BPD has no childhood-conduct-disorder requirement. Adult callous disregard (criterion 7) further supports.
source_refs: DSM-5-TR ASPD criteria A and C

options.text: Irritability and verbal aggression toward family members without any documented evidence of childhood conduct disorder or adult criminality
options.rationale: Irritability is part of both ASPD and BPD criteria; not specific. Would never be discriminating (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Articulate self-presentation and use of charm during interview without any documented childhood conduct disorder or adult exploitation history
options.rationale: Glibness/superficial charm is in Hare's PCL-R psychopathy construct but not DSM-5 ASPD criterion; raises probability but not most specific. Would be specific only in PCL-R framework (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: ASPD requires conduct disorder before age 15 PLUS adult disregard for others' rights, deceitfulness, impulsivity, irritability, recklessness, irresponsibility and lack of remorse. BPD centres on identity disturbance, fear of abandonment, idealisation/devaluation, emptiness and self-harm. The pre-15 conduct disorder marker is the most reliable discriminator.
key_points: ASPD diagnosis requires age >=18 AND childhood conduct disorder onset before 15
BPD features absent here: emptiness, abandonment fear, splitting, self-harm
Both have high substance use comorbidity
Forensic comorbidity is much higher in ASPD
Treatment options are limited; psychological interventions modest, no medication for the core disorder
explanation.pearls: Always ask about pre-15 behaviour (truancy, fire-setting, cruelty to animals, theft) when ASPD is on the differential - it is a hard DSM-5 criterion (DSM-5).

stem: A 45-year-old corporate lawyer is referred by his GP after his wife of 12 years left him last month. He attends reluctantly, stating he expects to be seen by 'the head of the department' and finds the waiting room beneath him. He describes his career in detail, refers to himself as one of the best litigators in the country and as misunderstood by colleagues who he believes envy him. He reports that his wife 'failed to appreciate his achievements' and that her departure has caused mild low mood but no anhedonia, no neurovegetative symptoms and no suicidal ideation. He has no history of self-harm, no chronic emptiness, no fear of abandonment, no impulsive risk-taking and no history of conduct disorder, criminal behaviour or callous disregard for others' rights. Collateral from his sister describes a lifelong (since early adulthood) tendency to demand admiration, exploit junior staff to advance his cases, and react to mild criticism with prolonged contemptuous anger. He drinks 8 standard drinks weekly and is on no regular medications.

data_table.Examination: Well dressed, expansive manner, reactive affect, no thought disorder, no perceptual disturbance, K-10 18.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, EUC, LFT, TSH all within reference range.

lead_in: Which DSM-5 personality disorder best fits this clinical pattern, and what is the key discriminator from a closely related diagnosis?

options.text: Histrionic personality disorder - excessive emotionality and attention-seeking; key discriminator from narcissism is dramatic emotionality without grandiose self-importance
options.rationale: Histrionic PD has dramatic, sexually provocative or shallow-affect attention-seeking; he is grandiose and contemptuous rather than dramatic/seductive. Would be correct in dramatic/seductive presentation (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Borderline personality disorder - identity disturbance, unstable relationships and self-harm; key discriminator is affective instability rather than grandiose self-importance
options.rationale: BPD requires identity disturbance, abandonment fear, splitting and impulsive self-damage - all absent. Sustained competent functioning is not BPD pattern. Would be correct in unstable identity/relationships pattern (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Antisocial personality disorder - exploitation, lack of remorse and disregard for others' rights; key discriminator is childhood conduct disorder before age 15
options.rationale: ASPD requires childhood conduct disorder (criterion C) and adult pattern of disregard for others' rights/laws; no childhood conduct history, no criminal pattern. Would be correct with conduct disorder before 15 (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Narcissistic personality disorder - grandiose self-importance, need for admiration, exploitativeness and rage at criticism; key discriminator from ASPD is absence of childhood conduct disorder
options.rationale: NPD meets >=5 of 9 criteria here: grandiose self-importance, fantasies of unlimited brilliance, belief he is 'special', requires excessive admiration, sense of entitlement, interpersonally exploitative, lacks empathy, envious or believes others envy him, arrogant/haughty. Differentiation from ASPD turns on absence of childhood conduct disorder and criminal behaviour; differentiation from histrionic turns on grandiosity vs dramatic emotionality.

options.text: Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder - preoccupation with orderliness, perfectionism and control; key discriminator is rigidity rather than admiration-seeking grandiosity
options.rationale: OCPD has rigid perfectionism, preoccupation with rules/lists, miserliness, over-conscientiousness; he shows grandiosity and contempt, not perfectionism. Would be correct in perfectionist pattern (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: Narcissistic personality disorder is a Cluster B personality disorder featuring grandiosity, need for admiration, sense of entitlement, exploitativeness, lack of empathy, and rage at perceived criticism. It is distinct from BPD (abandonment, emptiness) and ASPD (early conduct disorder, criminality).
key_points: NPD: grandiosity + need for admiration + lack of empathy across multiple contexts
Onset by early adulthood, stable over time
Often presents at crisis points (relationship breakdown, professional setback)
No specific pharmacotherapy; psychological therapy can be modestly helpful
explanation.pearls: NPD is often noticed by the team feeling devalued or treated as servants - countertransference is diagnostic data (RANZCP).

stem: A 38-year-old accountant is referred by his GP for psychiatric review at his wife's insistence. He works 11-hour days, refuses to delegate work because no one else does it correctly, keeps meticulous spreadsheets of household expenditure, and becomes irritable when family members leave items out of place. His wife reports he is rigid about routines, hoards old work documents 'for completeness', and is reluctant to spend money even when finances are comfortable. He sees these behaviours as appropriate professional standards and is not distressed by them; rather, he is troubled by his family's resistance to his system. He denies intrusive unwanted thoughts, denies ritualised compulsions performed to neutralise anxiety, and has no history of contamination fears, checking compulsions or symmetry-driven rituals. His mood, sleep, appetite and concentration are unremarkable and he has no history of self-harm. His MSE is unremarkable except for circumstantial speech and a markedly orderly presentation.

data_table.Examination: Punctual, suit pressed, papers arranged precisely, K-10 14, no anxiety on observation.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, EUC, LFT, TSH within reference.

lead_in: Which feature most strongly supports obsessive-compulsive personality disorder rather than obsessive-compulsive disorder?

options.text: Rigid preoccupation with order and control
options.rationale: Order and control feature in both OCPD and symmetry/ordering subtype of OCD; not strongest discriminator alone. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Hoarding of work documents at home
options.rationale: Hoarding can occur in OCPD, hoarding disorder and occasionally OCD; without ego-syntonic/dystonic context does not discriminate. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Reluctance to delegate tasks to others
options.rationale: Reluctance to delegate is OCPD criterion but can arise from anxiety in OCD; supportive not decisive. Would never alone be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Long working hours with high productivity
options.rationale: High productivity may occur in either OCPD or unaffected high-functioning workers; not DSM-5 criterion in isolation. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Behaviours felt as appropriate, not intrusive
options.rationale: OCPD behaviours are ego-syntonic - the patient sees them as correct standards and does not experience the intrusive obsessions or anxiety-driven compulsions of ego-dystonic OCD; this is the defining clinical discriminator (DSM-5).

explanation.summary: OCPD behaviours are ego-syntonic and pervasive across life domains; OCD obsessions are intrusive, unwanted and ego-dystonic, with anxiety-driven compulsions performed to reduce distress. Ego-syntonicity is the cleanest line in clinical practice.
key_points: OCPD: 4+ of preoccupation with detail/rules, perfectionism interfering with completion, workaholism, rigid morality, hoarding, reluctance to delegate, miserliness, rigidity/stubbornness
OCD: obsessions + compulsions, distressing and time-consuming
Insight is typically better in OCPD because the person doesn't see the problem
OCD responds to SSRIs + CBT with exposure-response prevention; OCPD does not have a specific treatment
explanation.pearls: If the patient is not distressed by their own behaviour, consider OCPD before OCD (RANZCP anxiety guidance).

subtopic_detail: Safety planning vs admission decision in BPD
stem: A 23-year-old woman with established BPD attends the emergency department after taking 8 paracetamol tablets following a fight with her current partner. This is her fourth low-lethality presentation in the past 6 months. Serum paracetamol is below the treatment line at 8 hours and LFTs are normal. On interview she is engaged, has agreed to handover of remaining medication to a relative, identifies her older sister as a strong protective factor able to stay with her tonight, and has a current outpatient DBT clinician who can see her tomorrow morning. She denies current plan or intent for further self-harm, denies psychotic features, and her K-10 is 28. She has previously had a 4-week voluntary admission that she describes as destabilising, with an increase in self-harm in the week after discharge. Her treating team has a documented care plan recommending brief, focused community management for low-lethality presentations.

data_table.Examination: Alert, reactive affect, no thought disorder, no perceptual disturbance, multiple healed and recent linear forearm scars.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol below Rumack-Matthew treatment line, ALT 22 U/L, INR 1.0, K-10 28.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge with a structured safety plan and next-day DBT review
options.rationale: For low-lethality chronic self-harm in BPD with adequate community supports, NHMRC and RANZCP recommend a structured safety plan (means restriction, identified supports, crisis numbers, early follow-up) and rapid return to the patient's usual psychological therapy; admission is not required when risk can be managed less restrictively (NHMRC BPD; LifeSpan/Black Dog safety planning).

options.text: Brief admission for 48 hours to monitor for further self-harm
options.rationale: Routine short admissions for chronic low-lethality self-harm in BPD are not recommended; NHMRC favours community management with brief admissions only when essential. Would be correct only for safety crisis not manageable in community (Source: NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Apply Mental Health Act involuntary admission criteria
options.rationale: Involuntary admission requires mental illness plus risk not manageable less restrictively; she has engaged voluntarily with safety plan and trusted clinician. Would never be correct in this scenario (Source: Mental Health Act).

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nocte and discharge
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy not recommended for core BPD; antipsychotic at discharge after low-lethality overdose adds metabolic risk without benefit. Would never be correct (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Refer for inpatient DBT programme as next-day transfer
options.rationale: AU DBT is predominantly outpatient/day-programme; inpatient DBT is not standard; her existing outpatient clinician should continue care. Would never be correct as standard (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: For chronic low-lethality self-harm in BPD with adequate supports, the evidence-based response is a structured safety plan and rapid return to outpatient psychological therapy. Prolonged or routine admissions can worsen outcomes and erode autonomy; involuntary care requires least-restrictive justification.
key_points: Safety planning (Stanley-Brown structure): warning signs, internal coping, social distraction, people to ask for help, professionals/crisis lines, means restriction
Means restriction is a high-yield intervention - handover of medications, removal of access to lethal means
Admission reserved for: acute change in pattern, high-lethality attempt, intent without supports
Comorbidities (MDD, substance use) may shift the threshold for admission
Document risk reasoning explicitly, including least-restrictive justification
explanation.pearls: Chronic vs acute-on-chronic risk: the threshold for admission rises when the pattern is chronic and low-lethality and falls when intent, lethality or means change suddenly (NHMRC BPD).

stem: A 78-year-old woman is admitted with a community-acquired pneumonia and started on intravenous benzylpenicillin and oral doxycycline. On day 2 the nursing staff call you because she is restless, climbing out of bed, picking at the bed sheets and intermittently shouting at staff she has known well during the admission. Her daughter, who lives with her, says her cognition was normal at baseline last week and she manages her finances independently. On interview her attention fluctuates: she can count backwards from 20 to 10 with multiple errors and her conversation drifts mid-sentence. She is not orientated to date or place. Six hours earlier on the morning ward round she had been calm and orientated. Vital signs show T 37.9, HR 102, BP 128/72, SpO2 95% on 2 L NP, RR 22. CXR confirms a right lower lobe consolidation. Sodium is 132 mmol/L, urea 8.2 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, WCC 14.2 x10^9/L.

data_table.Examination: Restless, picking at sheets, fluctuating attention, disoriented to date/place, no focal neurology, RLL crackles
data_table.Investigations: Na 132 mmol/L, urea 8.2, Cr 88, WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, CXR RLL consolidation
data_table.Medications: Benzylpenicillin IV, doxycycline PO, paracetamol PRN

lead_in: Which clinical tool finding best confirms a diagnosis of delirium in this patient?

options.text: MMSE score of 18 today compared with prior 28
options.rationale: MMSE detects cognitive impairment but cannot confirm delirium (does not capture acute onset, fluctuation or attentional disturbance specifically). Would be correct only as part of broader CAM-based assessment (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Acute onset and fluctuation PLUS inattention PLUS disorganised thinking or altered consciousness
options.rationale: The Confusion Assessment Method (CAM) is positive when there is (1) acute onset and fluctuation AND (2) inattention AND either (3) disorganised thinking OR (4) altered level of consciousness; all four features are present here, confirming delirium (ACSQHC Delirium Clinical Care Standard; Inouye CAM).

options.text: MoCA score of 17 with deficits across multiple cognitive domains
options.rationale: MoCA detects MCI better than MMSE but does not specifically diagnose delirium (no acute onset/fluctuation capture). Would never alone confirm delirium (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: GCS of 14 with mild reduction in eye opening
options.rationale: GCS measures consciousness and is insensitive to attentional and thinking disturbance characterising hyperactive delirium with normal arousal. Would be correct only for impaired conscious level (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Geriatric Depression Scale score of 9 of 15
options.rationale: GDS screens for depression in older adults; depression can mimic delirium but is not diagnosed by fluctuating attentional picture. Would be correct only for depression (Source: ACSQHC).

explanation.summary: Delirium is a clinical diagnosis using the CAM: acute onset and fluctuation PLUS inattention PLUS disorganised thinking OR altered level of consciousness. MMSE/MoCA/GCS are supportive but not delirium-specific. Search for precipitants - infection, hypoxia, medications, electrolytes, pain, constipation, urinary retention.
key_points: CAM = positive when 1+2 AND (3 OR 4)
Inattention is the most discriminating feature (test with backward counting, days of week reverse, serial 7s)
Hyperactive delirium: agitation, restlessness, hallucinations - most easily noticed
Hypoactive delirium: quiet withdrawal, drowsiness - frequently missed
Always reassess in older inpatients each shift
explanation.pearls: If the family says 'this is not how she normally is', screen for delirium with CAM before attributing behaviour to dementia (ACSQHC Delirium Clinical Care Standard).

stem: An 84-year-old woman is admitted from a residential aged care facility with a urinary tract infection. On day 3 of intravenous ceftriaxone the nursing staff are concerned that she is withdrawn, eating poorly, sleeping during the day and unable to engage with the ward physiotherapist. Her usual GP letter from 2 weeks ago documents independent ADLs with walking aid, no prior mood disorder, and stable cognition with an MMSE of 27 at her last GP review. On examination she is drowsy but rousable, slow to respond, attention is markedly impaired on backwards-counting, and her thinking is disorganised when she does engage. She has no thoughts of suicide and her affect is flat rather than clearly sad. Sodium is 128 mmol/L, urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, WCC 12.6 x10^9/L, CRP 84 mg/L. She is on paracetamol regularly and was started on oxycodone 5 mg PRN for hip pain two days ago.

data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, markedly impaired attention, disorganised thinking, flat affect, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Na 128 mmol/L, urea 12, Cr 102, WCC 12.6, CRP 84, MSU growing E. coli sensitive to ceftriaxone
data_table.Medications: Ceftriaxone IV, paracetamol regular, oxycodone 5 mg PRN (commenced day 1)

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence sertraline 25 mg daily for late-life depression
options.rationale: Acute onset, fluctuating attention and disorganised thinking is hypoactive delirium not depression; starting antidepressant first misses underlying medical cause. Would be correct in confirmed depression (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Commence haloperidol 0.5 mg BD for behavioural disturbance
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are reserved for delirium with severe agitation or distress risking harm; hypoactive presentations should not be routinely treated with antipsychotics. Would be correct only with severe agitation (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Stop oxycodone, correct sodium and treat the urinary infection
options.rationale: The CAM features are met for hypoactive delirium. The single most appropriate step is to identify and reverse precipitants: stop the newly added opioid, correct hyponatraemia with judicious fluid management, ensure ongoing UTI treatment, optimise environment, hydration, mobilisation and orientation (ACSQHC Delirium CCS).

options.text: Arrange CT brain to exclude an acute infarct
options.rationale: CT brain is for delirium with focal neurology, head injury, anticoagulation or unexplained drop in conscious level; routine imaging not first-line. Would be correct only with focal features (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Refer to consultation-liaison psychiatry for ECT consideration
options.rationale: ECT not first-line in this acute medical context; diagnosis is not depression and ECT is for severe treatment-resistant or psychotic depression. Would never be correct in delirium (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Hypoactive delirium is the most missed delirium subtype and is often mislabelled as depression. CAM features (acute onset, fluctuating attention, disorganised thinking or altered consciousness) point to delirium. Management is to identify and reverse precipitants - the PINCH-ME mnemonic (Pain, Infection, Nutrition, Constipation, Hydration, Medication, Environment) is useful.
key_points: Hypoactive delirium > hyperactive in prevalence and is associated with worse outcomes
Antidepressants do not treat delirium and may worsen confusion, hyponatraemia or anticholinergic burden
Common precipitants: opioids, anticholinergics, infection, electrolytes, urinary retention, constipation
Antipsychotics: lowest effective dose, only when distress or risk; never for hypoactive presentations routinely
Mortality and length of stay rise with delirium - prevention and early reversal are core geriatric care
explanation.pearls: Quiet withdrawn older inpatient is delirium until proven otherwise, not depression (ACSQHC Delirium Clinical Care Standard).

stem: A 79-year-old man is brought to his GP by his wife who reports a 12-month gradual decline in short-term memory with two recent episodes of getting lost in a familiar suburb, slowly worsening word-finding difficulty, and recent difficulty managing his medications and bills. He denies low mood, anhedonia or suicidal ideation. He sleeps well, eats adequately, and his interest in lawn bowls is preserved when his wife sets out the equipment for him. Over the past three months his cognition has plateaued without further sudden deterioration, his attention on serial 7s is mildly impaired but not fluctuating, and his MMSE today is 22 of 30 with deficits in delayed recall and orientation. Examination is unremarkable with no focal neurology. CT brain shows generalised cortical atrophy without infarcts or mass. Sodium, urea, B12, TSH, calcium, liver function, MSU and FBC are all within reference range. He is on perindopril and metformin only.

data_table.Examination: Pleasant, attentive at interview, mild word-finding pauses, MMSE 22/30, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Na 138, urea 5.2, Cr 82, B12 380, TSH 1.6, Ca 2.32, ALT 24, MSU negative, FBC normal, CT brain: generalised cortical atrophy
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily, metformin 500 mg BD

lead_in: Which feature most strongly favours dementia over either delirium or late-life depression?

options.text: Preserved interest in usual hobbies when supported
options.rationale: Preserved interest argues against major depression but does not distinguish dementia from delirium; delirium can have preserved interest. Would never be the discriminator (Source: Dementia Australia).

options.text: MMSE of 22 with deficits in delayed recall
options.rationale: Low MMSE shows cognitive impairment but cannot distinguish delirium from dementia or depression-related cognitive impairment alone. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: Dementia Australia).

options.text: Generalised cortical atrophy on CT brain
options.rationale: Cortical atrophy on CT is common in older adults and supports neurodegeneration but is non-specific; does not distinguish from delirium in comorbid case. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: RANZCR).

options.text: Gradual progression over 12 months without acute fluctuation
options.rationale: Dementia typically progresses gradually over months to years without acute onset or marked diurnal fluctuation in attention, distinguishing it from delirium and from pseudodementia of severe depression; this temporal profile is the strongest discriminator (Dementia Australia clinical resources; RACGP Aged Care Clinical Guide; RANZCP).

options.text: Wife's observation that he gets lost in familiar areas
options.rationale: Getting lost is suggestive but can occur in delirium with disorientation; temporal profile is more diagnostic than single deficit. Would never alone be the strongest discriminator (Source: Dementia Australia).

explanation.summary: Onset and course are the most reliable clinical features in distinguishing dementia (gradual, progressive, stable attention) from delirium (acute, fluctuating, impaired attention) and from depressive pseudodementia (subacute, mood-driven, often complaints of cognitive failure with effortful but accurate testing). Investigations help exclude reversible causes.
key_points: Dementia: gradual, months-years, attention typically preserved early
Delirium: acute, fluctuating, attention markedly impaired
Depression: subacute, mood symptoms primary, complains of memory more than tested
Dementia workup: FBC, EUC, LFT, TFT, B12/folate, Ca, glucose, MSU, CT or MRI
Mixed presentations are common - re-screen after delirium resolution
explanation.pearls: Anchor on the family timeline: 'When was he last himself?' - gradual months means dementia, hours-to-days means delirium (Dementia Australia).

stem: An 82-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer dementia is reviewed in her residential aged care facility after staff request medication for behavioural disturbance. Over the past two weeks she has become more agitated in the late afternoon, calling out for her late husband, attempting to leave the unit, and resisting personal care. There is no change in her usual medications, no fever days, and a recent MSU was negative. She is eating and drinking adequately and her sleep is broken with daytime napping. A pain assessment using the Abbey Pain Scale is 3 of 18, but staff have noticed she grimaces when transferred from chair to bed. The behavioural disturbance is not associated with risk of physical harm to herself or staff. Her family is willing to be involved with care.

data_table.Examination: Restless in late afternoon, calling for husband, mild grimace on transfers, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: MSU negative, FBC, EUC, CRP unremarkable, no fever
data_table.Medications: Donepezil 10 mg daily, paracetamol PRN, perindopril 4 mg daily

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Risperidone 0.25 mg BD for behavioural symptoms
options.rationale: Antipsychotics in BPSD are for severe distress or risk of harm not responsive to non-pharmacological measures; this patient has treatable pain hook and antipsychotics should follow first-line measures. Would be correct only after first-line failure with severe distress (Source: AMH).

options.text: Lorazepam 0.5 mg BD scheduled for agitation
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen confusion, falls and fracture risk in older adults with dementia; not first-line for BPSD. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Increase donepezil from 10 mg to 23 mg daily
options.rationale: Donepezil not licensed at 23 mg in Australia; dose escalation is not first response to BPSD. Would never be correct in AU (Source: AMH; TGA).

options.text: Citalopram 10 mg daily as adjunct for agitation
options.rationale: SSRIs (especially citalopram) have modest BPSD evidence but should follow non-pharmacological measures; citalopram over 20 mg in older adults raises QT risk. Would be correct only after first-line failure (Source: AMH).

options.text: Scheduled regular paracetamol plus structured environmental measures
options.rationale: First-line BPSD management is non-pharmacological with attention to unmet needs - pain, infection, constipation, hunger, fatigue, environment and meaningful activity; a likely pain contributor warrants scheduled analgesia plus orientation, family contact, predictable routine and meaningful engagement (Dementia Australia BPSD; RACGP).

explanation.summary: BPSD is a final common pathway with many drivers. First-line care is to look for and treat unmet needs (pain, infection, constipation, urinary retention, hunger, fatigue, sensory deficits, environmental triggers) and apply non-pharmacological strategies (routine, meaningful activity, family contact, orientation, music). Antipsychotics are reserved for severe distress or risk after non-pharmacological measures.
key_points: Pain is often missed in advanced dementia - use Abbey Pain Scale and trial scheduled analgesia
Late-afternoon agitation (sundowning) responds to light, routine, reduced demands
Antipsychotics in dementia: increased stroke and mortality - use lowest dose for shortest time
Benzodiazepines worsen confusion and falls
Cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine have only modest BPSD effects
explanation.pearls: If a person with dementia 'changes', screen for pain, infection, constipation and urinary retention before reaching for an antipsychotic (Dementia Australia BPSD).

subtopic_detail: Antipsychotic risk in elderly with dementia
stem: A 79-year-old man with moderate Alzheimer dementia has had three weeks of distressing persistent persecutory delusions that staff are stealing from him, with intermittent verbal aggression toward his wife. Non-pharmacological measures including environmental modification, regular toileting, scheduled analgesia for known knee osteoarthritis, family attendance and a structured daily routine have been trialled for two weeks with limited effect. There is no delirium screen positive: he is alert, attention is not fluctuating, and his usual cognitive performance has not changed over the past three months. He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidaemia and previous TIA. His current medications are perindopril, metformin, atorvastatin and aspirin. He has no QT-prolonging medications and his recent ECG showed QTc 432 ms. The family asks whether medication is now appropriate and how long any antipsychotic would be continued.

data_table.Examination: Alert, attention preserved, persistent persecutory delusions, no neurological deficit
data_table.Investigations: FBC, EUC, LFT, CRP, MSU all unremarkable, ECG QTc 432 ms

lead_in: Which prescribing approach best balances benefit and harm?

options.text: Risperidone 0.25 mg BD reviewed at 12 weeks for ongoing clinical need
options.rationale: Risperidone is the only antipsychotic with a specific Australian indication for short-term treatment of severe BPSD; recommended dose is low (0.25-1 mg BD) with mandatory review at no more than 12 weeks because of stroke and mortality risk (AMH; Dementia Australia BPSD; TGA risperidone PI).

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg nocte continued indefinitely without scheduled review
options.rationale: Olanzapine 10 mg is standard adult dose, not low-dose-elderly; indefinite continuation is contrary to guideline to review and deprescribe at 12 weeks. Would be correct only at lower dose with scheduled review (Source: AMH).

options.text: Haloperidol 2 mg BD for ongoing delusional content and agitation
options.rationale: Haloperidol has higher EPSE and QT risk in older adults; not first-line for BPSD, reserved for short-term severe delirium-related agitation. Would be correct only in short-term severe agitation (Source: AMH).

options.text: Quetiapine 100 mg BD as first-line treatment for psychosis
options.rationale: Quetiapine has weaker evidence in BPSD outside Parkinson dementia/DLB, and 100 mg BD exceeds cautious starting dose in older adults. Would be correct only at lower dose for DLB/Parkinson dementia (Source: AMH).

options.text: Lorazepam 1 mg TDS scheduled regularly for ongoing agitation
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen confusion, gait, falls and pneumonia risk in older adults; not appropriate for psychotic features. Would never be correct (Source: Choosing Wisely).

explanation.summary: Antipsychotics in dementia carry an increased risk of stroke and all-cause mortality. Use is restricted to severe BPSD with significant distress or risk after non-pharmacological measures, at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration, with mandatory review and deprescribing attempt within 12 weeks. Risperidone holds the only Australian indication.
key_points: Risperidone: 0.25-1 mg BD short-term for severe BPSD
Mandatory 12-week review and deprescribing attempt
Increased stroke and mortality in dementia is a class effect
Avoid haloperidol unless severe delirium and short-term
Avoid benzodiazepines for BPSD
explanation.pearls: If an antipsychotic is started for BPSD, put the deprescribing review on the diary the same day - not at 12 months (Dementia Australia BPSD; AMH).

stem: A 74-year-old man is brought to the GP by his daughter with 6 months of fluctuating attention and cognition, recurrent well-formed visual hallucinations of small children in his lounge room, and 4 months of mild bilateral cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia and a slow shuffling gait. He has had two unexplained falls in the past two months. He has no resting tremor of note. He has no prior psychiatric history, no alcohol or substance use, and his medications are perindopril and metformin only. Bloods, B12, TSH, sodium and CT brain are unremarkable. MMSE is 22 of 30 with prominent fluctuation between assessments two weeks apart. There are no features of stroke, no acute infectionation. The daughter asks what should be done about the visual hallucinations which are now distressing him in the evenings.

data_table.Examination: Bradykinesia, bilateral cogwheel rigidity, shuffling gait, fluctuating attention, well-formed visual hallucinations
data_table.Investigations: FBC, EUC, B12, TSH, MSU, CT brain all unremarkable, MMSE 22/30 (fluctuating)
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily, metformin 500 mg BD

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step for the distressing visual hallucinations?

options.text: Commence haloperidol 0.5 mg BD
options.rationale: Typical antipsychotics including haloperidol can produce severe neuroleptic sensitivity reactions in DLB (worsening Parkinsonism, severe sedation, autonomic instability). Would never be correct in DLB (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Trial rivastigmine titrated to 6 mg BD oral
options.rationale: Cholinesterase inhibitors (rivastigmine and donepezil) have evidence for cognition, fluctuation and visual hallucinations in dementia with Lewy bodies and are recommended first-line for neuropsychiatric symptoms over antipsychotics, given the neuroleptic sensitivity of DLB (AMH; Dementia Australia; RANZCP).

options.text: Commence risperidone 0.25 mg BD
options.rationale: Risperidone is licensed for BPSD generally but should be avoided in DLB due to severe neuroleptic sensitivity; low-dose quetiapine preferred if any antipsychotic required. Would be correct in non-DLB BPSD (Source: AMH).

options.text: Start levodopa-carbidopa at low dose
options.rationale: Levodopa can worsen visual hallucinations in DLB even though it may improve Parkinsonism; trade-offs must be weighed and not first response to hallucinations. Would be correct only with motor priority (Source: AMH).

options.text: Lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN for distress
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen falls and confusion in DLB and do not treat the underlying neuropsychiatric symptoms. Would never be correct (Source: Choosing Wisely).

explanation.summary: Dementia with Lewy bodies is the second most common neurodegenerative dementia and is characterised by fluctuating cognition, recurrent well-formed visual hallucinations, REM sleep behaviour disorder and Parkinsonism. Patients have marked neuroleptic sensitivity - antipsychotics (especially typicals and risperidone) can cause severe deterioration. Cholinesterase inhibitors are preferred for neuropsychiatric symptoms.
key_points: DLB core features: fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, Parkinsonism, REM sleep behaviour disorder
Avoid haloperidol and conventional antipsychotics; prefer quetiapine if antipsychotic essential
Cholinesterase inhibitors (rivastigmine, donepezil) - improve cognition and hallucinations
Levodopa can worsen hallucinations - weigh against Parkinsonism gain
Falls are common - review hypotension, deprescribe sedatives
explanation.pearls: Visual hallucinations + Parkinsonism + fluctuation in an older adult is DLB until proven otherwise - do not give haloperidol (Dementia Australia).

stem: A 72-year-old retired engineer presents to his GP at his wife's prompting with 9 months of subjective forgetfulness, including occasional missed appointments and a tendency to repeat questions in conversation. His wife agrees there has been a noticeable decline compared with two years ago. Crucially, he remains independent with all instrumental activities of daily living: he drives safely on familiar routes, manages the household finances and his self-administered medications, prepares meals and uses his computer for hobbies. He has no mood symptoms and his K-10 is 12. He has no fluctuation in attention, no hallucinations and no Parkinsonism. His MMSE is 28 of 30 with mild deficits in delayed recall, but a MoCA is 23 of 30, with deficits across delayed recall, visuospatial function and abstraction. Sodium, B12, folate, TSH, calcium, FBC and CT brain are within reference. He asks what the diagnosis is and what to do next.

data_table.Examination: Pleasant, attentive, mild word-finding pauses, no Parkinsonism, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: MMSE 28/30, MoCA 23/30, Na 139, B12 410, folate 18, TSH 1.8, Ca 2.34, FBC normal, CT brain mild atrophy

lead_in: Which classification and next step is most appropriate?

options.text: Major neurocognitive disorder - commence donepezil
options.rationale: Major NCD (dementia) requires significant functional impairment which this man does not have; cholinesterase inhibitors not licensed for MCI. Would be correct only with functional impairment (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Normal ageing - no review needed
options.rationale: Objective cognitive impairment beyond age expectation on MoCA with corroborated functional change excludes normal ageing. Would never be correct (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Mild cognitive impairment - monitor with annual cognitive review
options.rationale: MCI is defined as objective cognitive decline beyond age expectation with preserved functional independence; recommended management is structured monitoring (typically 6-12 monthly), cardiovascular risk optimisation, lifestyle (exercise, sleep, social engagement) and review for reversible factors (Dementia Australia; RACGP Aged Care Clinical Guide).

options.text: Vascular dementia - commence aspirin and statin
options.rationale: Vascular dementia requires evidence of cerebrovascular disease and stepwise decline; no infarcts on imaging and gradual course; already on statin. Would be correct with imaging-confirmed cerebrovascular disease (Source: RANZCR).

options.text: Subjective cognitive decline only - no investigations indicated
options.rationale: Subjective cognitive decline applies when complaint exists without objective deficit; MoCA shows objective impairment moving classification to MCI. Would be correct with normal MoCA (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: Mild cognitive impairment is the bridge between normal age-related cognitive change and dementia: objective cognitive impairment greater than expected for age and education, but preservation of instrumental activities of daily living (managing finances, medications, driving, cooking, shopping). The dementia threshold is crossed when IADL function declines - cognitive screen scores alone (MMSE, MoCA, ACE-III) cannot make this distinction. Annual MCI-to-dementia progression is around 10-15%, but a substantial proportion remain stable or revert to normal with reversible-cause treatment. Australian practice is to screen for and address reversible contributors (vitamin B12, TSH, depression, sleep apnoea, alcohol use, polypharmacy, anticholinergic burden, hearing and vision impairment), optimise vascular risk factors (BP, lipids, diabetes, smoking, exercise), encourage cognitive and social engagement, and monitor annually with cognitive testing and functional history (ideally with collateral from a partner or close family member). Cholinesterase inhibitors are not indicated in MCI - they are reserved for established dementia.
key_points: MCI vs dementia: function preserved vs significantly impaired
MoCA more sensitive than MMSE for MCI (cut-off ~26)
No medications licensed for MCI in Australia
Exercise, sleep, vascular risk control, cognitive engagement
Annual reassessment with caregiver collateral
explanation.pearls: Mild cognitive impairment is defined by objective cognitive impairment with preserved instrumental activities of daily living (IADLs); the dementia line is set by functional decline, not by cognitive screen scores. About 10-15% of MCI progresses to dementia per year (Dementia Australia; NIA-AA).

stem: A 79-year-old woman with mild Alzheimer dementia is admitted with a displaced intracapsular fractured neck of femur after a fall at home. The orthopaedic team plan a hemiarthroplasty within 48 hours. On interview she is alert and conversant, oriented to person and place, knows the year but not the date. She knows she has broken her hip and that the doctors want to do an operation to put it back together. She can repeat back, in her own words, that the operation involves replacing part of the bone, that risks include bleeding, infection and clots, and that without surgery she would be bed-bound with significant pain. She says she wants to have the surgery so she can walk again. Her MoCA today is 22 of 30 with deficits in delayed recall and visuospatial domains.

data_table.Examination: Alert, oriented to person/place, MoCA 22/30, conversant, no acute confusion
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118, EUC unremarkable, INR 1.0, ECG NSR, AP pelvis: displaced intracapsular NOF fracture
data_table.Medications: Donepezil 10 mg daily, perindopril 5 mg daily, paracetamol 1 g QID

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate determination of capacity for this surgical consent?

options.text: She lacks capacity because her MoCA is below 26
options.rationale: Capacity is not determined by cognitive test score; MoCA cut-off is for screening cognitive impairment, not capacity. Would never be correct (Source: state Guardianship Acts).

options.text: She lacks capacity because of her dementia diagnosis
options.rationale: Dementia diagnosis does not equal incapacity; many with mild dementia retain decision-specific capacity. Assumption of incapacity is contrary to AU principles. Would never be correct (Source: Guardianship Acts).

options.text: Defer to her son to provide substitute consent
options.rationale: Substitute decision-makers are engaged only when person lacks capacity; defaulting to family bypasses autonomy. Would be correct only after demonstrated lack of capacity (Source: Guardianship Acts).

options.text: She has capacity for this specific decision at this time
options.rationale: Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. She can understand, retain, weigh and communicate the information relevant to this decision in her own words; she meets the functional test for capacity for this surgical consent despite mild dementia (Australian Law Reform Commission; RACGP; respective state Guardianship Acts).

options.text: Request a Mental Health Act assessment for involuntary treatment
options.rationale: MHA does not authorise consent to surgical procedures; for incapable patients without advance directive or guardian, guardianship pathways apply. Would never be correct (Source: MHA).

explanation.summary: Capacity is decision-specific, time-specific, and presumed unless disproven. The functional test asks whether the person can understand, retain, weigh and communicate the information for the specific decision. Cognitive screening informs but does not determine capacity.
key_points: Functional test: understand, retain, weigh, communicate
Decision-specific - capacity for surgery can co-exist with incapacity for finances
Time-specific - reassess if delirium, intoxication or medication effects change
Diagnosis alone (dementia, mental illness) does not establish incapacity
If incapable: enduring guardian > tribunal-appointed guardian > responsible person under state law > medical practitioner emergency provisions
explanation.pearls: Document capacity assessment in the patient's own words; that record is the strongest medico-legal protection (RACGP).

stem: A 78-year-old woman is reviewed by her GP for a 10-week history of low mood, anhedonia, early morning waking, 4 kg weight loss, poor concentration and pervasive guilt about being a burden on her daughter. She has had passive thoughts of being better off dead with no plan or intent. She meets criteria for a moderate major depressive episode with K-10 of 34 and PHQ-9 of 17. She lives alone, mobilises with a walking stick after a previous hip fracture, has a documented falls risk and an annual fracture risk on FRAX. Her comorbidities are hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and stage 3a CKD with eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73 m2. Her current medications are perindopril, metformin, atorvastatin and hydrochlorothiazide. Sodium is 134 mmol/L, normal otherwise. She has no prior antidepressant trial. Her ECG QTc is 432 ms. After discussion of psychological therapy and lifestyle measures, she agrees to commence an antidepressant.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, mild psychomotor slowing, no psychotic features, gait with stick, K-10 34, PHQ-9 17
data_table.Investigations: Na 134, K 4.2, Cr 96, eGFR 52, ALT 22, TSH 1.6, ECG QTc 432 ms

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial antidepressant?

options.text: Amitriptyline 25 mg nocte titrated weekly
options.rationale: TCAs are avoided in older adults (anticholinergic load: cognition, falls, constipation, urinary retention) and overdose toxicity. Would never be correct as first-line (Source: AMH).

options.text: Citalopram 40 mg daily as initial dose
options.rationale: Citalopram doses above 20 mg in older adults not recommended (QT risk); 40 mg as starting dose is contraindicated. Would be correct only at start dose 10 mg (Source: TGA).

options.text: Venlafaxine XR 150 mg daily as initial SNRI
options.rationale: Venlafaxine has dose-dependent HTN and discontinuation effects; 150 mg starting dose in older adult with CKD and antihypertensives is too high. Would be correct only at lower starting dose (Source: AMH).

options.text: Mirtazapine 45 mg nocte to address sleep and appetite
options.rationale: Mirtazapine can be useful when insomnia/weight loss dominate but 45 mg is too high a starting dose in older adult (typical start 15 mg). Would be correct at 15 mg start (Source: AMH).

options.text: Sertraline 25 mg daily, monitor sodium and falls
options.rationale: Sertraline is a preferred SSRI in older adults given low drug-interaction profile, weak QT effect, and broad evidence in late-life depression. Start at half adult dose and monitor for SIADH/hyponatraemia (already 134 with thiazide co-prescription) and for falls/fractures (eTG Psychotropic; AMH; RANZCP Mood Disorders 2020).

explanation.summary: Late-life depression is treated with SSRIs as first-line; sertraline is commonly preferred in older adults given safety profile. Start low (often half adult dose), monitor for SIADH (particularly with thiazide co-prescription), falls, GI bleeding and discontinuation. Avoid TCAs and high-dose citalopram in this group.
key_points: First-line late-life depression: SSRI (sertraline commonly preferred)
Start at half the adult dose and titrate slowly
Monitor sodium at 1-2 weeks, especially with thiazide or carbamazepine
SSRI risks in older adults: SIADH, falls, GI bleeding, QT (citalopram)
Avoid TCAs and paroxetine
explanation.pearls: Always check baseline sodium and recheck at 1-2 weeks after starting an SSRI in an older adult on a thiazide (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

stem: A 31-year-old woman is reviewed at her 6-week postnatal visit. She had a normal vaginal birth of a healthy term infant; the pregnancy was planned and uncomplicated. Over the past 3 weeks she has experienced persistent low mood, anhedonia, broken sleep beyond what is explained by the baby's feeds, tearfulness, guilt about her mothering and difficulty bonding. Her EPDS is 16 with item 10 negative for self-harm. She has no prior major depressive episodes, no bipolar history, no psychotic features, and no current thoughts of harm to herself or the baby. She is exclusively breastfeeding and prefers to start with a psychological therapy before considering medication. She has a supportive partner and family, no current significant stressors beyond the role transition to parenthood, no relationship grief or recent bereavement. She works in clerical administration and has private health insurance covering psychology sessions.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, reactive affect, no psychotic features, bonded enough to feed and care for the baby, no thoughts of harm

lead_in: Which structured psychological therapy is best supported as first-line for her presentation per the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline?

options.text: Acceptance and commitment therapy as first-line for perinatal depression in this primiparous woman with mild-to-moderate symptom severity today
options.rationale: ACT has growing evidence in perinatal populations but not first-line per COPE for postnatal MDD; reserved as alternative if IPT/CBT unavailable. Would be correct only if first-line unavailable (Source: COPE).

options.text: Interpersonal therapy (IPT) - structured 12-16 sessions focusing on role transitions and interpersonal conflict, with the strongest perinatal-specific RCT evidence base
options.rationale: IPT has the strongest postnatal-specific evidence base (multiple RCTs including O'Hara 2000; Stuart manual); the role-transition focus is mechanistically matched to the parenthood transition that triggered her episode. COPE positions IPT and CBT as first-line for postnatal MDD, with IPT specifically called out for role-transition presentations.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline 2023
O'Hara et al RCT IPT postnatal

options.text: Long-term psychodynamic psychotherapy with weekly sessions over 12-18 months focusing on early attachment patterns and developmental themes
options.rationale: Insufficient RCT evidence in postnatal depression; not first-line. Would never be correct as first-line (Source: COPE).

options.text: Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR) targeting any incidental trauma history rather than the perinatal mood presentation itself
options.rationale: EMDR is for PTSD, not postnatal MDD without trauma history. Would be correct with PTSD (Source: Phoenix Australia).

options.text: Dialectical behaviour therapy with weekly group skills training plus individual sessions over a 12-month treatment programme as first-line
options.rationale: DBT is for BPD and chronic self-harm, not postnatal MDD. Would be correct in BPD (Source: NHMRC BPD).

explanation.summary: Therapy choice matches the clinical pattern. IPT and CBT lead in perinatal depression. ACT has its strongest evidence in chronic pain and treatment-resistant anxiety. DBT is reserved for BPD/self-harm. EMDR is indicated for PTSD. Psychodynamic therapy is a longer-term option where access permits.
key_points: IPT - perinatal depression, grief, role transitions
ACT - chronic pain, treatment-resistant anxiety, values-based behaviour change
CBT - broad first-line for anxiety, OCD, PTSD (trauma-focused), MDD
DBT - BPD and recurrent self-harm
EMDR - PTSD
Match therapy to formulation, not just diagnosis label
explanation.pearls: If the clinical formulation centres on role transition or interpersonal loss, IPT is often the highest-value match (RANZCP Perinatal).

subtopic_detail: Motivational interviewing and stages of change
stem: A 52-year-old man attends his GP at his wife's request after a recent admission with alcoholic gastritis. He drinks 8-10 standard drinks daily, has done so for at least 10 years, and acknowledges that his drinking 'might be a bit too much' but feels he could cut down on his own if he wanted to. He has not made any concrete plans to reduce, has not removed alcohol from the house, has not engaged with treatment, and does not yet identify himself as having a problem requiring help. He has no current severe withdrawal symptoms (no tremor, no autonomic instability), no current suicidal ideation, AUDIT 22. PMH: T2DM, hypertension, macrocytosis on FBC. The GP plans a longer follow-up consultation to address the drinking through a structured behaviour-change framework.

data_table.Examination: Mild epigastric tenderness, no encephalopathy, no asterixis, no jaundice
data_table.Investigations: AUDIT 22, MCV 102 fL, GGT 220 U/L, ALT 64 U/L, HbA1c 7.4 per cent

lead_in: Which stage of change does this man most clearly demonstrate, and what is the best-matched evidence-based intervention plan today?

options.text: Precontemplation - provide brief structured information about risks; do not raise treatment options today as he is not yet ready to change
options.rationale: Precontemplation = no awareness; he has acknowledged 'might be a bit too much', which is awareness without commitment. That is contemplation. Would be correct only without any acknowledgement (Source: Prochaska/DiClemente).

options.text: Preparation - schedule a quit date within 30 days, start naltrexone today, and provide a brief intervention focused on the AUDIT score and end-organ damage
options.rationale: Preparation requires intention to act within 30 days and concrete planning; he has neither. Jumping to quit date risks engagement loss. Would be correct only with stated intention and planning (Source: Prochaska).

options.text: Contemplation - motivational interviewing for ambivalence, brief intervention with FRAMES, and OFFER naltrexone or acamprosate given AUDIT and end-organ damage
options.rationale: Stage matching: he acknowledges the problem (past precontemplation) but is ambivalent and not yet planning concrete action (not yet preparation). MI is stage-matched. Importantly, stage matching does NOT mean withholding evidence-based pharmacotherapy: AUDIT >=20 and end-organ damage independently justify offering naltrexone or acamprosate now, framed as supports for the change he is contemplating, not as imposed treatments.

options.text: Action - initiate naltrexone today, refer to a relapse-prevention group, and arrange weekly clinic follow-up for the first three months
options.rationale: Action requires active behaviour change within last 6 months; he has not yet started. Would be correct only after change initiated (Source: Prochaska).

options.text: Maintenance - relapse-prevention planning even though he has not yet achieved any reduction in drinking or made a quit attempt yet
options.rationale: Maintenance follows 6+ months of sustained behaviour change; not applicable. Would be correct only after sustained change (Source: Prochaska).

explanation.summary: The transtheoretical (Prochaska-DiClemente) stages of change model frames behaviour change as movement through precontemplation (no awareness or intent to change), contemplation (aware, ambivalent), preparation (planning concrete steps within 30 days), action (active change for less than 6 months), and maintenance (sustained change beyond 6 months), with relapse looping back to any earlier stage. Motivational interviewing is the matched intervention for contemplation and early preparation: it elicits the patient's own change talk, works with ambivalence by reflection rather than confrontation, and follows the FRAMES structure (Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu of options, Empathy, Self-efficacy). Common clinician errors include premature problem-solving (moving to action plans before the patient is ready), confrontation, and giving unsolicited advice - all push the patient back into precontemplation. Pharmacotherapy for alcohol use disorder (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram) is appropriate in action stage with adequate motivation and abstinence goals.
key_points: Precontemplation: not considering change
Contemplation: aware of problem, ambivalent, no firm plan in 30 days
Preparation: plan within 30 days
Action: changes initiated in past 6 months
Maintenance: sustained for >6 months
MI core skills: OARS - Open questions, Affirmations, Reflections, Summaries
explanation.pearls: In motivational interviewing, sustain talk (the patient arguing against change) is a clinician error - the technique requires rolling with resistance through reflection, not pushing harder. The FRAMES brief intervention is the evidence-based outpatient package: Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu, Empathy, Self-efficacy (eTG Addiction).

subtopic_detail: Psychodynamic vs supportive vs CBT matching
stem: A 42-year-old man is reviewed in a community mental health team after a third presentation in 12 months with recurrent moderate depressive episodes, prominent self-criticism, longstanding difficulty in close relationships, and a pattern of leaving jobs when feedback feels critical. He has had two previous structured 12-session CBT courses with modest short-term improvement that has not been sustained, and he describes finding the homework-driven structure of CBT useful but feels that 'something deeper isn't shifting'. He is psychologically minded, articulate, and curious about understanding patterns from his upbringing. He has no current suicidal ideation, no substance use, and is currently on sertraline 100 mg with partial response. He works part-time and his health fund covers extended psychology sessions. He is asking what to try next that is not another medication change.

data_table.Examination: Reactive affect, no thought disorder, no perceptual disturbance, K-10 24
data_table.Investigations: FBC, EUC, LFT, TSH all unremarkable

lead_in: Which structured psychological therapy is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Another 12-session course of cognitive behavioural therapy
options.rationale: Two prior CBT courses with partial transient response; repeating same modality without changing formulation unlikely to produce durable change. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders).

options.text: Supportive counselling alone
options.rationale: Supportive counselling is adjunctive but not structured first-line therapy for recurrent depression with relational patterns. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Dialectical behaviour therapy
options.rationale: DBT is first-line for BPD and recurrent self-harm, not recurrent MDD with relational patterns without personality disorder features. Would be correct in BPD (Source: NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Time-limited psychodynamic psychotherapy
options.rationale: Short-term psychodynamic psychotherapy has evidence in recurrent depression with prominent interpersonal patterns and self-criticism, especially in psychologically minded patients who have plateaued on CBT; it explores recurrent relational patterns and unconscious contributors (RANZCP Mood Disorders 2020; Cochrane Short-term Psychodynamic).
source_refs: Cochrane: Short-term psychodynamic therapy for depression

options.text: Acceptance and commitment therapy
options.rationale: ACT has evidence in chronic pain and treatment-resistant anxiety; reasonable alternative but psychodynamic better matched for recurrent depression with relational patterns. Would be correct as alternative (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Treatment-resistant or recurrent depression with prominent interpersonal and self-critical themes is one of the clearest indications for short-term psychodynamic psychotherapy. Choice between CBT, IPT, ACT and psychodynamic approaches is matched to formulation and prior response, not loaded onto a single modality.
key_points: Recurrent depression with relational patterns - consider time-limited psychodynamic
Treatment-resistant anxiety / chronic pain - ACT
BPD / recurrent self-harm - DBT
Perinatal depression / grief - IPT
Primary anxiety disorders / OCD / PTSD - CBT (often trauma-focused for PTSD)
explanation.pearls: When the patient says 'something deeper isn't shifting' after good CBT trials, that is a clinical cue toward a relational-pattern-focused therapy (RANZCP Mood Disorders 2020).

sources.label: Cochrane: Short-term psychodynamic therapy for depression

subtopic_detail: Complex elderly - delirium superimposed on dementia + capacity
stem: An 84-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer dementia (premorbid MMSE 22 documented 6 weeks ago) is admitted from her home with a urinary tract infection and dehydration after her daughter found her drowsy. On day 4 of intravenous ceftriaxone the infection has clinically resolved with CRP falling from 156 to 24 mg/L, MSU now sterile, and sodium corrected from 122 to 137 mmol/L. Her attention this morning is back to baseline on backwards-counting, her CAM is negative, her behaviour has settled, and the family-nurse and OT report she is at the cognitive level documented before this admission. Today she tells the team she wants to go home and refuses to engage with proposed residential aged care assessment. Her daughter, who is the documented enduring guardian, disagrees and is pushing strongly for nursing home placement.

data_table.Examination: Alert, attention preserved on backwards-counting, MMSE 22 (back to documented baseline), CAM negative
data_table.Investigations: Na 137, urea 6.4, Cr 88, CRP 24, MSU sterile, FBC normal
data_table.Medications: Donepezil 10 mg daily, perindopril 5 mg daily

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Override her refusal because the daughter is her enduring guardian
options.rationale: Enduring guardians make substitute decisions only when person lacks capacity for that particular decision; if patient has capacity, refusal stands. Would be correct only after capacity assessment confirms incapacity (Source: Guardianship Acts).

options.text: Apply Mental Health Act involuntary admission to keep her in hospital
options.rationale: MHA addresses mental illness with risk not manageable less restrictively; does not authorise placement decisions for cognitive impairment without mental illness. Would never be correct (Source: MHA).

options.text: Discharge immediately because the delirium has resolved
options.rationale: Discharge requires assessment of safe destination, supports, and (if capacity in question) documented capacity assessment; immediate discharge bypasses this. Would never be correct (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Continue antibiotics for another 7 days while debate continues
options.rationale: Infection has resolved; ongoing antibiotics expose to harm without benefit and do not resolve discharge question. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Reassess decision-specific capacity for the discharge destination
options.rationale: Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. The delirium has now resolved (CAM negative, returned to baseline cognition); a fresh functional assessment for the specific decision of discharge destination is required before any substitute decision-maker is engaged or any application made to the Civil and Administrative Tribunal of the relevant state (ALRC; RACGP; state Guardianship Acts).

explanation.summary: Delirium-superimposed-on-dementia is the geriatric trap: once the delirium has resolved, capacity must be reassessed at the new baseline before any substitute decision-maker steps in. Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific; an enduring guardian acts only when capacity is absent for the relevant decision. The least-restrictive principle applies throughout.
key_points: Delirium often unmasks underlying dementia but resolves to baseline once the precipitant is treated
Always reassess capacity at the new baseline before placement decisions
Enduring guardian acts only when capacity is absent for the decision in question
Risk of harm in the community is balanced against autonomy under the least-restrictive principle
If capacity is uncertain, the relevant state guardianship tribunal arbitrates
Document the capacity reasoning in the patient's own words
explanation.pearls: Family wishes do not override capable refusal - they are weighed and discussed, not enacted by default (Australian Law Reform Commission).
